The NOCS-inspired approach is compared to the direct regression approach in terms of achieving better error reduction (53% and 74% reduction on chairs and CARLA) while being more general. The direct regression baseline achieves a similar performance to Pix2NeRF’s encoder despite using a more expressive transformer architecture, suggesting that the main bottleneck lies in the pose representation itself and not in the architecture.

The NOCS-inspired approach is evaluated in pose estimation where the mean rotation angle between predicted and ground-truth orientations is compared, showing a significantly better error reduction (53% and 74% reduction on chairs and CARLA, respectively) while being more general.

The direct regression approach is compared to Pix2NeRF’s encoder, showing similar performance despite using a more expressive transformer architecture. The comparison suggests that the main bottleneck lies in the pose representation itself and not in the architecture.

The NOCS-inspired approach is mentioned alongside the NOCS-based model, with the former achieving better error reduction and faster convergence due to better incorporation of equivariances to certain geometric transformations.

The direct regression approach is compared to the NOCS-based model in terms of convergence speed, where it is observed that the NOCS-based model converges much faster due to better incorporation of equivariances to certain geometric transformations.
The NOCS framework incorporates equivariances to geometric transformations, while the baseline method has to learn them from the data.
The baseline method has to learn equivariances to geometric transformations from the data.
